metadata.title: How Anthropic Uses Claude Code
metadata.source: CWC Workshop — How We Claude Code (Anthropic internal workflow)

beats.narration_text: Hearing how Anthropic uses Claude Code internally makes me want to copy the parts that actually change how a team ships, not just the demo tricks. Pick one concrete habit from their workflow, like keeping a CLAUDE.md or delegating a whole subtask to the agent: (1) explain the specific friction it removes on a real codebase, (2) show me a before-and-after of a task done with and without it, (3) tell me which of their practices tends to backfire for a solo developer versus a large team.

props.command: Hearing how Anthropic uses Claude Code internally makes me want to copy the parts that actually change how a team ships, not just the demo tricks. Pick one concrete habit from their workflow, like keeping a CLAUDE.md or delegating a whole subtask to the agent: (1) explain the specific friction it removes on a real codebase, (2) show me a before-and-after of a task done with and without it, (3) tell me which of their practices tends to backfire for a solo developer versus a large team.
props.runningText: Analyzing: CWC Workshop — Anthropic Product Workflow
output: Phase 1: Claude interviews the team to produce a product spec
Phase 2: Four divergent HTML mockup directions before any code
Phase 3: Component fixtures with verifiable DOM contracts

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Three phases. Brainstorm, design, verify. The order matters. You do not write code in phase one. You do not commit to a design in phase one. Phase one is a structured interview: Claude asks the team about the product they actually want to build. The output is a spec. That spec feeds phase two.

artifactLines: Brainstorm — Claude interviews the team; output is a product spec
Design — four divergent mockup directions; human picks one
Verify — component fixtures with DOM contracts; machine-testable from day one
No code is written in phase one
No design is committed in phase one

beats.narration_text: Phase one produces a product spec through a Claude-conducted interview. The prompt is public — you can run it. Claude asks about goals, users, constraints, edge cases. What comes out the other side is a brief, not a list of features. The distinction: a brief has a point of view. A feature list is just inventory.

props.artifactHeading: The prompt that replaces the meeting.
artifactLines: 1. The prompt is public — you can run it yourself
2. Claude asks about goals, users, constraints, and edge cases
3. Output: a brief with a point of view — not a feature list
4. A brief has a point of view; a feature list is just inventory
5. The spec from this interview feeds phase two

beats.narration_text: Phase two: generate four divergent design directions as static HTML mockups. Not wireframes. Not sketches. Fully rendered pages you can open in a browser. Four different philosophies, four different visual languages. The human picks one. Nothing is implemented yet. This is the only point in the workflow where the decision is aesthetic.

artifactLines: 1. Generate four divergent design directions as static HTML mockups
2. Not wireframes — fully rendered pages you open in a browser
3. Four different philosophies, four different visual languages
4. Human picks one; nothing is implemented yet
5. This is the only point in the workflow where the decision is aesthetic

beats.narration_text: Phase three converts the chosen direction into verifiable architecture. Each component gets a fixture: the exact DOM state it should produce under defined inputs. Run bun verify and get a pass or fail for every component. The AI can build to the spec; the spec is machine-readable. The gap between the mockup and the working product is a test suite.

artifactLines: 1. Each component gets a fixture: exact DOM state under defined inputs
2. Run bun verify — get pass or fail for every component
3. The spec is machine-readable; the AI can build to it
4. CI-testable from day one
5. Gap between mockup and working product = a test suite

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. Paste this and fill in your project: I want to build — then describe it. Before you write any spec or code, interview me. Ask the minimum questions — maybe five or six — to understand what I actually want. Then produce a one-page brief: what we're building, what success looks like, and the first three concrete steps. Watch what the spec looks like before a line of code is written. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: I want to build [describe your project]. Before you write any spec or code, interview me — ask the minimum questions you need (maybe five or six) to understand what I actually want. Then produce a one-page brief: what we're building, what success looks like, and the first three concrete steps.
output: interviews before it touches the spec
produces a one-page brief from the conversation
identifies the three first concrete steps
